Okay, so what is cognitive neuroscience? So the book's called The Biology of the Mind, okay?
It really is about trying to understand the mind, so how we think, right?
That's kind of a mental construct. But using neuroscience material, like using facts or information or methods that probe the brain as a way to understand how the mind operates.
And it's primarily, I'd say there's four primary disciplines that contribute to cognitive neuroscience.
There's neurology, and neurology, again, is a branch of medicine.
So a neurologist is gonna have an MD. And they're usually, they're mostly interested in pathology of the nervous system, right?
You go to see a neurologist when you might have had a stroke or if you're developing memory problems and they wanna make a diagnosis of Alzheimer's or something like that.
Actually, most people who go to a neurologist have back pain.
That's probably 90% of the cases a neurologist sees. But there's also a whole part interested about the function and the pathology of the nervous system.
So a neurologist is interested in what's wrong and what we can do to cure it.

But of course, they're studying people with brain disorders. And that turns out to be a rich source of material for making inferences about how different parts of the brain come together to allow us to do something like learn new material or orient our attention or perform skilled movements.
Then there's this massive, the neurosciences. Again, a person who's a neuroscientist probably has a PhD, not an MD.
So they're a researcher oriented towards research. Comes in all sorts of varieties if you.
If you go to the annual meeting of neuroscience, it's one of the largest conferences in the world.
There'll be like 35,000 people there studying every possible thing.
Some of them are studying anatomy, neuroanatomy. Some are studying physiology.
So anatomy's about the structure. Physiology's about the function, what makes a neuron become active and so forth.
Neurochemistry about neurotransmitters and so forth. All sorts of different things fall under this giant umbrella of neuroscience.
And that's really focusing on the mechanisms of the nervous system.
What makes cells fire? How do they communicate? How do networks come together and so forth?
So that's all again on the neuroscience side, but the cognitive neuroscience part is that really cognitive neuroscience is really just a branch or a version of cognitive psychology.
And cognitive psychology is really interested about how does the mind process information, right?
How do we use language? Or how do we memorize things and so forth?

These are the topics we'll cover. Cognitive psychology can exist without any study of the brain, but cognitive neuroscience came into kind of existence in the sense when people recognized that we have tools that can study, say, human brain function and that they might be informative for understanding how the mind processes information.
And it's kind of grown up hand in hand with computer science, cognitive psychology.

And cognitive science and computer science in a sense kind of all emerged together in the 1950s and through the present there as people realized that computer systems in some sense, not in other senses, but in some sense are another information processing system.
And there may be parallels between how computers process information and how people process information, animals process information.
There's probably striking differences there, but we could also then build computer models to try to mimic human behavior.
Now there's whole branches of artificial intelligence that don't care about how humans process information.
They just want a computer that's gonna do it efficiently.

But there's also large branches of computer science and AI that are interested in trying to say, can we use these tools as ways to understand how the brain organizes, how such complex behavior can emerge from something that is composed of many, many billions of tiny little elements.
Now, of course, now the word cognitive neuroscience, you'll see this in chapter one.
They talk about one of the authors, Mike Gazzaniga, talks about coining the term back in the 1970s and so forth.
But that doesn't mean like, you know, out of the blue he had this idea like, hey, let's study cognitive psychology by using neuroscience methods.

There's a long, long history here where people have understood from thousands of years now that there's some important relationship between this part, the structure underneath our skull here, and all the sort of mental facilities that we have.
And maybe its most prominent antecedent was in a branch of neurology that was called behavioral neurology.
And there was just really the emphasis, it's really the emphasis that's changed.
In behavioral neurology, the emphasis was about thinking about how are different parts of the brain associated with different tasks.

And the most dramatic example is the work starting in the 19th century that pinpointed that, hey, there's something special about the left side of the brain that's critical for language function differently than what's happening in the right hemisphere.

And that came about, again, through behavioral neurology. They might not have called neurologists at the time, but observations, for instance, seeing that people who had head trauma that affected the left side of the brain might have difficulties with language to a much greater degree than people had trauma from damage to the right side of the brain.
Many of these early traumatic events might have been from a war.
Of course, people were having strokes then, too, but we didn't have imaging tools to see what part of the brain was damaged in strokes, but there were different ways people could make inferences.

So again, the neurologist might give us these observations, and here's sort of a classic diagram from kind of the heyday of behavioral neurology, and you still get pictures like this pointing to different parts of the brain and saying, what are the functions associated with them?
So the occipital, back of the brain here, that seems to be really important for vision.

That's certainly true. Motor function associated up here, hippocampus with memory, and again, like I said, if we had the left and the right side of the brain separately, I'm sure there'd be an arrow pointing to the left side saying language, and there'd be an arrow pointing to the right side that might say visual-spatial processing and so forth.
This is all, of course, really important. It's very useful for a neurologist.

They'll know that, hey, if a person wakes up in the morning, has trouble speaking, and they go take a brain scan and see that they have a lesion, that they've had a rupture of a blood vessel in the left side of the brain, they'll know that this person may have long-term problems with language.
They take the same individual and image of the right side of the brain, and they see, oh, the damage was on the right side of the brain.
I mean, they don't take an image, take the image of the whole brain, but they see the damage.
the right side, they might know that this person's probably going to recover.

Their language problems are probably relatively transient due to the fact that they've had this massive sort of insult to the dynamics of the brain, but that the recovery is going to be quite good for one side versus the other.

So that's useful, right? If you're a behavioral neurologist, you want to make a diagnosis, you want to come up with a treatment plan, but the issue is that in terms of understanding, that's what I'm saying, understanding how language works, saying something like that the left side of the brain is more important than the right side of the brain for language actually tells you very little about language function itself, right?

And so that's where the behavioral neurologist limitation was, this focus on the general task, whereas the cognitive neuroscience idea was that we might have models of how language works or we might have models of how motor control works, and we can then test those models by trying to relate it to different parts of the brain and so forth, okay?

So really the question is about explaining how, just sort of linking tasks to different parts of the brain doesn't really tell you about how something like language works, knowing that the basal ganglia shrivels up atrophies in people and then they develop Parkinson's disease doesn't tell you much about motor control.
We see that these people have difficulty producing movements, but we don't get any real insight into what that part of the brain is doing that contributes to coordinated movement.

That's sort of the nature of cognitive neuroscience. So cognitive psychology, again, is really interested in that how question, it wants to analyze how, what are the different operations, mental operations, that are required for a person to either produce a skilled movement or to be able to communicate with another individual.
And so there were emphases always on these mental operations, right, to have language we need to understand the sounds.
We can talk about that in terms of the mental operations required.
We can also then talk about what parts of the brain are involved.
But again, the cognitive psychologists can do this by just thinking about the mental operations.
